content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administration 
•• 
Executive/Legel 
Enforcement 
322-6<Ul 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6.0U4 
Mr. Russ Lesser 
City Councilmember 
Manhattan Beach 
c/o Winds & McClaughry 
Accountancy Corporation 
P.O. Box 87 
Long Beach, CA 
90801 
Dear Mr. Lesser: 
322·5660 
322·5901 
November 15, 1982 
Re: Telephone Advice Request, 
Our No. A-82-187 
Thank you for your telehone call of November 8, 1982, 
requesting our advice. 
This letter will confirm that 
advice. 
FACTS 
You are a city councilmember in the City of t-'lanhattan 
Beach. 
You are a shareholder in a professional account-
ancy corporation, holding 9% of the stock. 
The firm has 12 
shareholders (partners), 100 employees and 5, 000 clients. 
Alexander Haagen is a developer who has developed property 
in Mannattan Beach in the past (Manhattan Village Shopping 
Center). 
He may develop property in Manhattan Beach again 
in the future, although he currently has no projects 
pending in tne jurisdiction. 
Mr. Haagen's operations are in need of certain 
accountancy services and he has approached your firm (Winds 
& McClaughry) for those services. 
You will not personally 
perform these services and will receive no direct payments 
from Mr. Haagen or his organization. 


Hr. Russ Lesser 
November 15, 1982 
Page 2 
QUESTION 
You have asked whether Mr. Haagen's use of your firm 
for his accountancy services will create any potential 
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.* 
Analysis 
The Political Reform Act (The "Act") provides that a 
public official may not make, participate in making, or use 
his official position to influence any governmental deci-
sion in which he knows or has reason to know that he has a 
financial interest. 
Section 87100. 
A financial interest 
is defined by Section 87103 as follows: 
An official has a financial interest in a decision 
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally, on: 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than loans 
by a commercial lending institution in the regular 
course of business on terms available to the 
public without regard to official status, aggre-
gating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in 
value provided to, received by or promised to the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 
Income is defined in Section 82030(a) to mean, among 
other things, the following: 
Income of an individual also includes a pro rata 
share of any income of any business entity or trust in 
which the individual or spouse owns, directly, 
indirectly or beneficially, a 10% interest or greater. 
Thus, if your ownership interest in your firm was 10% 
or more, Mr. Haagen would be a source of income to you, and 
if your pro rata share of his gross payments to the firm 
exceeded $250 during the 12 month period preceding any 
decision which would have a material financial effect upon 
*Government Code Sections 81000-91014. 
All statutory 
references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 


Mr. Russ Lesser 
Novemoer 15, 1982 
Page 3 
him, you would have a possible conflict. 
However, the 
facts here are that you only hold a 9% interest in the 
firm. 
Therefore, the payments by Mr. Haagen to the firm 
are not imputed to you. 
The firm is your source of income, 
not the client. 
Since Mr. Haagen is not a source of income to you, even 
if a decision would foreseeably affect Mr. Haagen, it would 
create no conflict of interest for you under the Act. 
On 
the other hand, since the firm is a source of income to 
you, a decision which would have a material financial 
effect on the firm would create a conflict. 
Therefore, if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision as to 
Mr. Haagen would result in a material financial effect upon 
your firm, there would be a conflict. 
However, given the 
size and the diversity of the firm, this seems a remote 
possibility. 
I trust that this letter has satisfactorily responded 
to your request for advice. 
Should you have any further 
questions regarding this letter, please do not hesitate to 
call me again. 
My number is 916/322-5901. 
REL: km 
~{~tf.~7 i--
Counsel 
I 
Legal Division

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city councilmember who holds a 9% stake in an accountancy firm has a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act when a property developer seeks accounting services from the firm.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists because the councilmember's 9% ownership interest falls below the 10% threshold required to impute client income. Although decisions affecting the firm itself could theoretically create conflicts, the firm's size and diversity make a material financial effect on the firm from this client highly unlikely.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Standard advice letter with clear FACTS, QUESTION, ANALYSIS, and implicit conclusion sections. Document ID reference A-82-187 confirms this is advice letter #187 from 1982.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked whether Mr. Haagen's use of your firm 
for his accountancy services will create any potential 
conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act.*

CONCLUSION: Since Mr. Haagen is not a source of income to you, even if a decision would foreseeably affect Mr. Haagen, it would create no conflict of interest for you under the Act. On the other hand, since the firm is a source of income to you, a decision which would have a material financial effect on the firm would create a conflict. Therefore, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision as to Mr. Haagen would result in a material financial effect upon your firm, there would be a conflict. However, given the size and the diversity of the firm, this seems a remote possibility.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises a Manhattan Beach city councilmember that his accountancy firm's provision of services to a property developer does not create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, since his 9% ownership stake is below the 10% threshold for imputing client income.
